Parties Involved: Knights of Labor, Workingmen's Party, general population
Significance: One of the first general strikes in the US, part of the Great Railroad Strike of 1877
Outcome: Significant civil unrest, fires, and destruction in the city of Pittsburgh, involvement of over 100,000 workers nationwide

Parties Involved: United Brotherhood of Teamsters, Employers' Association of Chicago, striking workers, police, strikebreakers
Impact: Weakened support for unions nationwide
Significance: Second-most deadly labor dispute in 20th-century American history, significant weakening of public support for unions

Impact: Defied assumptions of conservative trade unions, exposed poor mill conditions, investigation of wool trust
Duration: Over two months, from January to March 1912
Significant Event: Killing of striker Anna LoPizzo, leaders arrested, sending children away, violence at train station
Influence: Challenged the notion that immigrant, female, and ethnically divided workers couldn't be organized, enhanced IWW reputation

Outcome: Ended after five months with approximately 1,850 strikers arrested, including IWW leaders Bill Haywood and Elizabeth Gurley Flynn
Participants: Largely immigrant factory workers from southern and eastern Europe

Parties Involved: United Mine Workers of America, Alabama coal mine operators, UMW president John L. Lewis, UMW vice-president Van Bittner
Outcome: Significant defeat for the union and organized labor in Alabama, failed union recognition, continued use of convict labor until 1926
Main Demands: Union recognition, implementation of wage award decided by Bituminous Coal Commission
Challenges: Racial divisions, divide along racial lines, propaganda campaign by coal operators
Convict Labor: Continued use of convict labor by major operators under abominable conditions

Impact: Unionization of West Coast ports in the US
Influence: Rise of industrial unionism in the 1930s through Congress of Industrial Organizations

Parties Involved: International Longshoremen's Association, President Joe Ryan, 23 major shipping companies, NAACP, unaffiliated longshore unions in New Orleans
Impact: Strike failed, significant violence occurred, New Orleans maintained dock operations throughout the strike period
Significant Events: Strikes in major Gulf Coast ports, failed strike goal for ILA control of dock operations in New Orleans, significant violence during the strike

Parties Involved: Striking miners, company guards
Significance: Sparked by a broader miners strike for higher wages and an improved working condition, representing a time of tension and conflict in the Butte mining community
Outcome: Death of Tom Manning went unpunished, exacerbating tensions and the perception of injustice among the miners

Parties Involved: 10,000 armed coal miners, 3,000 lawmen, strikebreakers, coal mine operators, United States Army represented by the West Virginia Army National Guard
Impact: Temporary declines in United Mine Workers membership, improvements in membership, and working conditions

Parties Involved: Local coal miners, allies, Baldwin-Felts Detective Agency
Casualties: 7 detectives, 3 townspeople including mayor Cabell Testerman
Impact: Matewan massacre, escalation of tensions, battle in Pocahontas Coalfield mining district
Significance: Shootout in Mingo County, support for the union, clash between miners and detectives

Parties Involved: Chicago Police, crowd of around 10,000 men, women, and children
Significance: Part of the broader Great Railroad Strike of 1877, marked by tension between authorities and striking workers
Outcome: Police ran out of ammunition and retreated, demonstrating the challenges of maintaining order during the strike
Notable Events: Crowd gathered at Halsted Street viaduct, police fired at crowd, engagement lasted half an hour

Parties Involved: 7,000 building-trades workers, 5,000 Polish laborers, National Guardsmen, Governor Jeremiah M. Rusk
Casualties: 7 dead, including a thirteen-year-old boy, several injured
Significance: Demanding enforcement of an eight-hour workday, workers met with violence when gathering near Milwaukee Iron Company rolling mill in Bay View
Commemoration: Annual event held on the first Sunday in May to honor those killed during the incident

Incidents of Violence: Several violent incidents during the strike period
States Affected: Colorado, Illinois, Ohio, Pennsylvania, West Virginia

Impact: First labor dispute with federal government intervention, significant wage increases, union growth, improved conditions
Outcome: Miners received a 10% wage increase and reduced workdays to nine hours, owners received a higher price for coal, and did not recognize the union
Demands: Higher wages, shorter workdays, recognition of the union
Government Role: U.S. Federal Government and President Theodore Roosevelt intervened as neutral arbitrator
Wage Increase: Miners received a 10% raise in wages

Parties Involved: Union miners, Western Federation of Miners, mine operators, Pinkerton or Thiel operatives, military
Casualties: 2 murders, destruction of non-union mining facility, multiple homes, and outbuildings, military occupation
Primary Demand: Unionization of non-union mines, higher union wage scale
Impact/Outcome: Formation of the Western Federation of Miners, unionization of most mines by 1894, pro-union sentiment in the area, violent response to unionization efforts, continued labor-management confrontations in the region
trigger: Coeur d'Alene, Idaho labor confrontation of 1899

Parties Involved: Labor union miners, mine owners, Pinkerton agent, Western Federation of Miners
Outcome: Formation of the Western Federation of Miners due to the violent response to the strike
Demands: Restoration of wages, reduction of work hours, grievances on board and lodging costs
trigger: Coeur d'Alene, Idaho labor strike of 1892

Parties Involved: Western Federation of Miners, mine owners, Colorado state government, Colorado National Guard
Demand: Return to 8-hour workday without pay reduction

Parties Involved: Colorado state militia, striking coal miners
Significance: First Columbine massacre, dispute over use of firearms, company town dynamics
Outcome/Impact: Increased tensions between miners and authorities, public outcry and investigations into use of force

Parties Involved: Industrial Workers of the World (IWW) union, local authorities, vigilantes, law enforcement
Impact: Highlighted the brutal suppression of labor rights and influenced labor organizing efforts in the region
Significance: Marked tensions in Pacific Northwest labor history, showed confrontation between labor and business forces

Parties Involved: Unemployed auto workers, Dearborn Police Department, Ford Motor Company security guards
Casualties: Four workers shot to death, more than 60 injured, with a fifth worker dying later due to injuries
Impact: Contributed to the unionization of the U.S. auto industry
Significance: Part of the chain of events leading to the unionization of the U.S. auto industry
Organizing Body: Unemployed Councils, project of the Communist Party USA

Parties Involved: Galloway Lumber Company, striking unionized mill workers, Brotherhood of Timber Workers, Lumber Workers Industrial Union, Industrial Workers of the World
Casualties: 4 dead, including union leader Asbury Decatur Hall, and around 50 wounded
Significance: Crucial event in Louisiana-Texas Lumber War of 1911-1912, part of the effort to unionize sawmill workers in the region
Outcome: Limited success for unionizing the mill workers at Grabow, Louisiana, but larger success for the LWIU from 1917 to 1924

Parties Involved: Railroad workers, local populations, unofficial militias, National Guard, federal troops, private militias organized by railroads
Location: Martinsburg, West Virginia and spread to other cities in New York, Pennsylvania, Maryland, Illinois, and Missouri
Impact: Workers not represented by trade unions; labor unrest led to disruptions in various cities; workers burned down and destroyed rail facilities and rolling stock
Significant Events: Strikes in multiple cities, supported by about 100,000 workers; intervention of federal troops led to suppression by early August
Reason: Baltimore and Ohio Railroad cut wages for the third time in a year
Aftermath: Labor continued to organize into unions; cities built armories to suppress further unrest; improvements in workers' benefits and conditions were implemented, such as the founding of the Employee Relief Association in 1880

Parties Involved: United Mineworkers of America, Southern Illinois Coal Company, union miners, non-union workers, mine guards, superintendent
Events Leading Up: Owner disregarded strike agreement, hired non-union workers to produce and ship coal, union miners shot striking workers, resulting in gunfight

Parties Involved: Amalgamated Association of Iron and Steel Workers, Carnegie Steel Company, private security agents
Significance: Pivotal event in U.S. labor history, major defeat for the union of strikers, setback for unionizing steelworkers
Workforce Demographics: Wages dropped, expansion of workforce, inclusion of unskilled workers
Working Conditions: Wage decreases, advanced production technology, material-handling innovations
Public Perception: Strikers persecuted, wages dropped, defeat for the union

Parties Involved: Indianapolis Traction & Terminal Company, Amalgamated Street Railway Employees of America, city police, rioters, Indiana National Guard
Casualties: 2 strikebreakers, 4 union members killed, numerous injured
Impact: Forced city under martial law, involvement of Indiana National Guard, rioters demanding military leave the city
Significance: Breakdown in public order, severe interruptions of statewide rail transportation, 1913 city elections affected

Parties Involved: Luzerne County sheriff's posse, striking immigrant anthracite miners
Location: Lattimer mine near Hazleton, Pennsylvania, United States
Casualties: At least 19 miners killed, scores more wounded
Impact: Highlighted exploitation of immigrant mine workers, led to increased awareness and reforms within the industry
Significance: Turning point in United Mine Workers history

Impact: Forced miners to reassess tactics and union philosophy, shift towards radical politics and militant policies
Outcome: Major defeat for the union, leading to the WFM leaving the AFL, shift towards revolutionary socialism
Union: Cloud City Miners' Union, Local 33 of the Western Federation of Miners
Reason for Strike: Underpayment, mine owners paying less than $3.00 per day

Parties Involved: Colorado National Guard, private guards, coal miners, John D. Rockefeller Jr.
Casualties: Approximately 21 killed, including miners' wives and children
Impact: Watershed moment in American labor relations, public outrage leading to investigations by Congress
Significance: Seminal event of the 1913–1914 Colorado Coalfield War, deadliest incident of the strike, heightened violence throughout Colorado for ten days

Parties Involved: Teamsters, police, Communist League of America
Impact: Catalyst for the rise of industrial unionism in the 1930s through the Congress of Industrial Organizations
Significance: Paved the way for the organization of over-the-road drivers and the growth of the Teamsters labor union

Parties Involved: United Mine Workers union, Henry Clay Frick, National Guard
Significance: Formed only the previous year, organized by United Mine Workers union, leading to fatalities and a historical marker erected in 2000
Background: Strike for higher wages and an eight-hour work day

Impact: Sparked a radicalized generation of lumber workers leading to more industry-wide strikes
Duration: Over three and a half months
Outcome: Partial achievement of demands, repercussions felt for decades
Organizing Union: Northwest Council of Sawmill and Timber Workers Union
Significant Event: Industry-wide paralysis of Northern California, Oregon, and Washington lumber industry

Parties Involved: Striking coal miners, coal operators
Impact: Prelude to subsequent labor-related conflicts in West Virginia, including the Battle of Matewan and Battle of Blair Mountain
Number of Casualties: Around 50 violent deaths, many more indirectly caused by starvation and malnutrition

Parties Involved: Strikers, private security agents, Pennsylvania State Police
Significance: Largest and most significant industrial labor dispute in Pittsburgh since 1892 Homestead strike, precursor to the Great Steel Strike of 1919
Working Conditions: Primitive, industrial peonage for immigrant workers, plant known as "The Last Chance" and "The Slaughterhouse"
Public Perception: Men persecuted, robbed, slaughtered, wives abused, deaths in plant averaged about one per day

Parties Involved: American Railway Union, Pullman Company, main railroads, labor unions, federal government
Significance: Shaped national labor policy during economic depression, turning point for US labor law
Outcome: Strike and boycott shut down much of nation's freight and passenger traffic, US labor policy impact
Working Conditions: Reductions in wages, absence of democracy, paternalistic company control, excessive rates, house ownership issues
Public Perception: Workers' grievances included lack of democracy, company control, high rates, house ownership rights

Parties Involved: Strikers, Pennsylvania State Militia, federal troops
Impact: Order restored by federal troops, sheriff blamed for lack of public order
Cause of Violence: Arson and riots, Long Depression of 1873–1879
Context: Great Railroad Strike of 1877, Long Depression of 1873–1879

Parties Involved: Streetcar Carmen's Union, United Railroads, James A. Farley, Mayor Eugene Schmitz, Edward Robeson Taylor, Thomas Mooney
Impact: Collapse of the strike, undermining of labor's political position, Mooney's attempt to reorganize the union
Outcome: Strike officially abandoned in mid-February, support only from the Daily News, workers' tree fell to obstruct tracks during strike, blockade of tracks by workers, injured strikebreaking motorman
Significant Events: Bloody Tuesday shootout, indictment of Mayor Schmitz and Patrick Calhoun, collapse of labor's political position
Legal Actions: Schmitz found guilty of extortion, dissolution of Carmen's Union Local 205

Organizer: Cannery and Agricultural Workers' Industrial Union (CAWIU)
Main Crops: Cherry, grape, peach, pear, sugar beet, tomato, cotton
Violent Tactics: Eviction of strikers, deputization of growers, attacks on workers by employers
Ethnic Demographics: 80% Mexican workers involved in cotton strikes
Significant Casualties: Dolores Hernàndez, Delfino D'Ávila, Pedro Subia
Legal Repercussions: CAWIU organizers arrested under California Criminal Syndicalism Act

Parties Involved: Railroad workers, local militia, state and federal troops
Outcome: Strikers returned to work with no concessions, several arrests and subsequent trials, formation of a battalion of the Pennsylvania National Guard
Root Causes: Long Depression following Panic of 1873, economic downturn, wage cuts, widespread unemployment

Parties Involved: St. Louis Transit Company, Amalgamated Street Railway Employees of America, St. Louis Metropolitan Police Department
Significant Events: Consolidation of streetcar companies, firing of workers, rioting, disruption of service
Companies Involved: St. Louis & Suburban Railway, St. Louis Transit Company

Impact: Attempt to organize the U.S. steel industry post-World War I
Organizing Union: Amalgamated Association of Iron, Steel and Tin Workers
Significant Event: Collapse of the strike on January 8, 1920
Influence: Weakened union faced challenges following technological advances and previous strikes

Parties Involved: Sugar cane workers, Knights of Labor, state militia, planters
Casualties: At least 35 killed, rumored total toll in the hundreds
Impact: Passage of discriminatory state legislation disenfranchising black workers
Significance: Largest strike in sugar cane industry, first conducted by formal labor organization
Outcome: Defeat of sugar workers, decades-long halt in organizing efforts

Parties Involved: United Mine Workers of America, Chicago-Virden Coal Company, armed detectives, African-American strikebreakers, striking mine workers
Casualties: 5 detectives/security guards killed, 8 striking mine workers killed, 5 guards wounded, 30+ miners wounded, 1 black strikebreaker wounded, engineer shot in the arm
Impact: Transformation of Virden into a sundown town, expulsion of black miners from the county, shift in labor dynamics, reflection of racial and labor tensions in the area
Significance: Reflects labor union tension and racial violence, led to expulsion of black miners from Macoupin County, part of a series of fatal conflicts reflecting social tensions of the time
Outcome: Settlement with almost every operator by Illinois District of UMWA, recruitment of African-American miners as strikebreakers by Chicago-Virden Coal Company

Parties Involved: Steel Workers Organizing Committee, Republic Steel Management, Youngstown police
Impact: Escalation of the strike into a violent encounter, increased tension between workers and management
Significance: Intense class battle during Little Steel strike, involvement of steelworkers' wives in union protest
